summary: The article reports on new Chinese export controls on rare earth materials and technologies specifically targeting advanced chipmaking (14nm logic chips and 256-layer memory) and military AI. It features commentary from industry observer Xiang Ligang, who claims that China has largely overcome its semiconductor equipment bottlenecks and is shifting from a defensive to an offensive posture in the 'chip war' with the US.
main_claims: China's new export controls on rare earths indicate that the country has resolved its semiconductor equipment bottlenecks.
Beijing is now shifting from a defensive to an offensive strategy in the global chip rivalry.
China has developed world-class semiconductor equipment that may soon surpass foreign alternatives.
Export controls on rare earths are intended to complicate foreign production of advanced lithography systems.
evidence_or_facts: China produces approximately 70% of mined rare earth output and over 80% of refined materials.
New export rules (effective Dec 1) require case-by-case review for exports related to logic chips <= 14nm and memory chips >= 256 layers.
Specific restricted materials include neodymium, praseodymium, samarium, dysprosium, and related magnetic materials/sputtering targets.
China also announced separate export controls on lithium battery and graphite anode materials.
rhetorical_frames: 'Chip war' as a strategic struggle for technological dominance.
state_and_policy_content: Ministry of Commerce and Customs announced export controls on rare earths, lithium battery materials, and graphite anode materials.
Implementation of a 'case-by-case review' system for exports to sensitive industries (advanced chips and military AI).
capital_accumulation_content: Mention of the expansion of production capacity and strengthening of manufacturing in the chip industry.
what_is_missing_for_theory_evaluation: Detailed data on the actual production costs or profit margins of these new Chinese chip-making machines.
Information on the actual market share or independent verification of Xiang Ligang's claims regarding the 'resolution' of bottlenecks.
Analysis of the relationship between state subsidies and the private capital accumulation of the firms producing this equipment.
Specifics on how these controls affect the global supply chain's value distribution.
